title: Algorithm Engineering
description: How real hardware shapes fast code — pipelines, caches and memory.

lectures.title: Lecture 02 · CPU Architecture 1
lectures.description: Flip through the pipeline — branching, memory & caches.

cards.question: Why care how the CPU works if your algorithm's Big-O already looks good?
cards.answer: Because constant factors matter in real life. Two programs with the same Big-O can differ hugely in speed, and misunderstanding the CPU can hide an enormous constant factor — a 'smart' Python program can lose to a well-tuned C++ one.

cards.question: What is the CPU 'pipeline', in plain terms?
cards.answer: An assembly line for instructions. Different instructions sit at different stages (fetch, decode, execute…) at the same time, so no stage sits idle.

cards.question: What is the single goal behind all the CPU's pipeline tricks?
cards.answer: Avoid waiting. Keep every part of the CPU busy instead of standing around doing nothing.

cards.question: What does 'superscalar' mean?
cards.answer: The CPU can work on several independent instructions at once — like having 4 workers on the line instead of 1.

cards.question: What is 'out-of-order' execution?
cards.answer: If a later instruction is ready while an earlier one is stuck, the ready one can jump the queue. The final results still come out in the correct order.

cards.question: What is 'speculation' in a CPU?
cards.answer: The CPU guesses what's coming next and starts early, before it's fully sure. If the guess was wrong, it throws that work away.

cards.question: Roughly how many instructions can a modern CPU juggle at once?
cards.answer: Hundreds 'in flight' at any moment. It looks chaotic, but the output still matches your written program.

cards.question: What is latency?
cards.answer: How long one instruction takes from start to finish. (Like: how long a single pizza takes to bake.)

cards.question: What is throughput?
cards.answer: How many instructions finish per unit of time at full speed. (Like: with many ovens, a fresh pizza comes out every few minutes.)

cards.question: Does the pipeline make a single instruction finish faster?
cards.answer: No — latency stays about the same. What it improves is throughput: many instructions finish in quick succession.

cards.question: What is a 'branch'?
cards.answer: An if / decision point where the next instruction depends on a condition (e.g., 'if green, go straight, else turn').

cards.question: Why are branches a problem for the pipeline?
cards.answer: The CPU races ahead and reaches the fork before it knows the answer, so it doesn't know which instructions to load next.

cards.question: How does the CPU cope with not knowing which way a branch goes?
cards.answer: It predicts (guesses) the outcome and speculatively runs ahead based on that guess.

cards.question: What happens when a prediction is right vs wrong?
cards.answer: Right → no time wasted, smooth flow. Wrong (misprediction) → it throws away the speculative work and restarts — an expensive stall.

cards.question: Which branches are cheap and which are slow?
cards.answer: Predictable branches are basically free. Random, coin-flip branches inside a hot loop cause massive slowdowns.

cards.question: How can the CPU predict branches so well?
cards.answer: It spots patterns — 'taken the last 100 times', repeating sequences — and learns from nearby branches. On normal code it's right over 95% of the time.

cards.question: What kinds of branches are hard to predict?
cards.answer: Ones taken about 50% of the time at random, or patterns too long/complex for the predictor to catch.

cards.question: What's the main trick to fix an unpredictable branch?
cards.answer: Branchless code — get the answer with math / conditional moves instead of an 'if', so there's no branch to mispredict.

cards.question: Why not just make everything branchless?
cards.answer: Well-predicted branches are already cheap, and branchless code often does extra work (computes both paths). It only helps when both paths are cheap AND the branch is truly unpredictable.

cards.question: What's the big speed mismatch inside a computer?
cards.answer: The CPU is extremely fast, but fetching data from main memory (RAM) is very slow — hundreds of cycles of waiting.

cards.question: What are caches, and why do they exist?
cards.answer: Small, very fast memory right next to the CPU. They hold data you're likely to need so the CPU doesn't wait on slow RAM.

cards.question: How do the cache levels (L1, L2, L3, RAM) compare?
cards.answer: Closer to the CPU = faster but smaller. L1 is tiny and fastest; RAM is huge but slowest. Goal: keep needed data in the fast, close caches.

cards.question: When the CPU asks for one value, what does the cache actually fetch?
cards.answer: A whole cache line — a chunk of neighboring data (~16 ints), not just the one value — betting you'll want the neighbors soon.

cards.question: What is spatial locality?
cards.answer: If you use one piece of data, you'll probably use the data right next to it soon (like reading left to right).

cards.question: What is temporal locality?
cards.answer: If you use a piece of data, you'll probably use that same data again soon.

cards.question: What is a prefetcher?
cards.answer: A helper that spots predictable access patterns (like scanning straight through memory) and loads the data early, before you ask.

cards.question: In the matrix experiment, why is reading row-by-row fast?
cards.answer: Data is stored row by row, so reading in that order fully uses each fetched cache line before moving on — no waste.

cards.question: Why is reading a matrix column-by-column slow?
cards.answer: Each jump lands in a different cache line, so the CPU fetches a whole chunk but uses only one value and throws the rest away — over and over.

cards.question: What is 'striding', and why can it hurt?
cards.answer: Jumping through memory in big steps instead of neighboring items. Big strides waste most of each cache line and stop the prefetcher from guessing the pattern.

cards.question: What's the key takeaway about memory access patterns?
cards.answer: Predictable, sequential access is fast; scattered, jumping access is slow — even for the exact same result.

cards.question: Which data structures are cache-friendly vs not?
cards.answer: Packed ones (arrays / vectors) are great. Scattered, pointer-chasing ones (linked lists, tree maps) are usually slow because the CPU chases data all over memory.

cards.question: What's the idea behind 'Structure of Arrays vs Array of Structures'?
cards.answer: Array of Structures is cleaner to read as a programmer; Structure of Arrays (each field in its own tight array) is often faster because you load only the data you actually use.

cards.question: What can the compiler optimize for you automatically?
cards.answer: Low-level tricks — simple branchless code, swapping slow operations for fast ones, removing repeated work, vectorizing loops.

cards.question: What can the compiler NOT do for you?
cards.answer: The big decisions — it won't change your data structure, your memory layout, or your algorithm. That's your job.

cards.question: What is the optimization hierarchy, in order?
cards.answer: 1) Correctness first, 2) pick the right algorithm, 3) pick cache-friendly data structures & layout, 4) then measure and fine-tune things like branch prediction.

cards.question: What's the golden rule before optimizing anything?
cards.answer: Measure first. The CPU does so much clever stuff that your guesses about what's slow are often wrong — let real numbers guide you. (And never measure an unoptimized build.)

lectures.title: Lecture 03 · CPU Architecture 2
lectures.description: Optimizing for real hardware — data layout, SIMD & parallelism.

cards.question: In what order should you optimize code?
cards.answer: Correctness first → right algorithm → good data/memory layout → only then micro-tricks like SIMD and branch prediction.

cards.question: AoS vs SoA — what's the difference?
cards.answer: AoS (Array of Structures) keeps each object's fields together (cleaner code). SoA (Structure of Arrays) stores separate lists per field (often faster).

cards.question: Why is SoA often faster than AoS?
cards.answer: Memory is read in chunks (cache lines). Packing needed values together means each chunk is full of useful data instead of skipped-over junk (striding).

cards.question: When is AoS perfectly fine?
cards.answer: When you use <mark>most fields</mark> of each object anyway — then nothing in the cache line is wasted.

cards.question: Why are new/malloc calls a hidden performance trap?
cards.answer: Allocating is <mark>far more expensive</mark> than normal operations — like ordering a new box from the store every time instead of reusing one.

cards.question: How do you fix allocating inside a hot loop?
cards.answer: Allocate once before the loop and reuse it (e.g. clear() a vector to keep its buffer), or reserve() capacity / use an object pool.

cards.question: What are 'hidden allocations'?
cards.answer: Accidental copies of containers or temporaries — often because C++ passes objects by value by default. Profile to catch them.

cards.question: What does SIMD mean and do?
cards.answer: Single Instruction, Multiple Data — one instruction applies the same operation to many values at once, like a cashier with 8 hands scanning 8 items in one beep.

cards.question: Should you usually write SIMD by hand?
cards.answer: No — it's complex and platform-dependent. Let the compiler do it (auto-vectorization); go manual only as a last resort after measuring.

cards.question: Most common reason the compiler doesn't vectorize?
cards.answer: Missing optimization flags — you need -O2/-O3 turned on (and sometimes flags telling it the hardware, e.g. AVX2, is available).

cards.question: What code patterns block vectorization?
cards.answer: Messy control flow (if/else, non-inlined calls), complex access patterns, and inter-iteration dependencies.

cards.question: What is an inter-iteration dependency?
cards.answer: When a step needs the previous step's result (a chain), so steps can't run at the same time. Example: v[i] = v[i-1]*w[i] is blocked.

cards.question: Why can float sums be hard to vectorize?
cards.answer: Reordering float additions can slightly change the result, so the compiler won't reorder unless allowed. Float add is commutative but not strictly associative.

cards.question: std::accumulate vs std::reduce for summing floats?
cards.answer: accumulate is strict left-to-right (can't reorder → no SIMD). reduce may reorder (→ can vectorize).

cards.question: What is memory alignment and why care?
cards.answer: Data should sit at a <mark>'nice' address</mark> (like parking a bus only in a bus-shaped spot). Misalignment hampers vectorization and causes slow loads that split cache lines.

cards.question: Practical trick to shrink a struct?
cards.answer: Reorder fields (big types first) to reduce padding — the same data can go from 40 bytes to 24 bytes for free.

cards.question: Why do we need parallelization now?
cards.answer: Per-core speed and frequency stopped growing (~2005); CPUs add more cores instead, so you must use many at once.

cards.question: What does Amdahl's Law tell us?
cards.answer: Only the parallelizable part speeds up; the <mark>part that must run alone caps total speedup</mark> — even with infinite cores. (Chefs share chopping, but one solo cake limits the meal.)

cards.question: The two fundamental hurdles of parallelization?
cards.answer: Synchronization (coordinating shared data) and shared resources (memory bandwidth, power, heat, network).

cards.question: What is the memory bandwidth limit?
cards.answer: A <mark>shared pipe to memory</mark>. Once it's full, more threads don't help — like many people sharing one water fountain.

cards.question: When is a task likely bandwidth-limited?
cards.answer: Reading lots of memory once, in a predictable linear scan, more data than cache, with light computation. Then a few threads already max it out.

cards.question: Best strategies to reduce synchronization?
cards.answer: Have each thread <mark>work its own chunk and combine at the end</mark>; keep critical sections short; hand out big-enough tasks.

cards.question: What is false sharing?
cards.answer: Threads write to different variables that share one cache line, so each write forces the others to refresh — like two people sharing one notebook page.

cards.question: Does false sharing cause wrong answers, and how do you fix it?
cards.answer: No — results are correct, just secretly slow. Fix by putting each thread's data on its own cache line (e.g. alignas(64)).

cards.question: Why can CPU utilization mislead you?
cards.answer: Busy-waiting (spinning) looks like 100% usage but does no useful work. Prefer 'effective time' plus wait/spin time.

cards.question: Low-level vs high-level parallelism?
cards.answer: Low-level = parallelize one task (parallel sum/sort). High-level = run whole different strategies at once (many seeds, different algorithms) — usually better.

lectures.title: Lecture 04 · Fundamental Data Structures
lectures.description: Why the data structure — not the Big-O — decides real speed.

cards.question: What's the one-line message of the whole lecture?
cards.answer: The data structure is the performance story. Same Big-O can be 100× apart in practice — because of cache behaviour and allocations, not asymptotics.

cards.question: What's the default linear structure, and why?
cards.answer: std::vector. It's contiguous, predictable, and cache-friendly — reach for something else only when the workload clearly demands it.

cards.question: Why is std::list usually much slower than std::vector?
cards.answer: A vector is contiguous, so the prefetcher streams ahead (one fetch → 8 elements). A list scatters nodes, so every hop risks a main-memory miss (~100× a cache hit).

cards.question: When does std::list actually win? (all three at once)
cards.answer: 1) Many splices, few traversals; 2) iterator stability (pointers must survive edits elsewhere); 3) elements expensive to move. If you can't name all three → use vector.

cards.question: What about std::deque?
cards.answer: Avoid it. It's a map of pointers to blocks — every iterator step risks crossing a block boundary. Prefer a vector with a read index as a queue (contiguous, one allocation).

cards.question: What is small-buffer optimization?
cards.answer: Store a few elements inside the object itself (on the stack), avoiding a heap allocation while small. Use absl::InlinedVector, boost::small_vector, llvm::SmallVector.

cards.question: Before using a hash map, which cases have a better tool?
cards.answer: Dense integer keys → index a std::vector directly. Ordered queries → sorted container. Tiny N (≲100) → sorted vector + linear scan (cache beats asymptotics).

cards.question: Why avoid std::unordered_map?
cards.answer: The standard mandates chaining — it's a linked list in disguise. Every insert allocates a node; every lookup chases a pointer. ~2× slower on hits, 3–4× on misses.

cards.question: What is open addressing and why is it faster?
cards.answer: Store (key,value) inline in one array; collisions probe adjacent slots. One allocation, and a hit/miss usually stays in the same cache line. Downside: wide values → fewer slots per line.

cards.question: What clever trick do Swiss tables add?
cards.answer: They split tiny metadata from the data. A single SIMD instruction checks 16 slot-tags at once; only matches pay to read the full key-value pair — fast regardless of value width.

cards.question: Default hash map + main tuning knobs?
cards.answer: Use ankerl::unordered_dense, absl::flat_hash_map, or boost::unordered_flat_map. reserve() before bulk inserts, leave load factor alone, and use a good hash for non-trivial keys.

cards.question: When do you actually need a sorted container?
cards.answer: Only when order is part of the query: range queries, predecessor/successor, ordered iteration, order statistics, DB indexes. If it's just point lookup → hash map.

cards.question: Default sorted structure, and its one weakness?
cards.answer: Sorted std::vector + std::lower_bound — build once, binary-search many. Weakness: mid-insert is O(N) (one insert into a million-key vector shifts megabytes).

cards.question: Why avoid std::map?
cards.answer: It's a red-black tree with per-node malloc — basically a std::list with a sort order, plus rebalancing on every insert. Slower than absl::btree_map on every workload.

cards.question: For balanced trees, where does the real cost come from?
cards.answer: From tree height, not node work. A million-key binary tree is ~20 levels = up to 20 cache misses per lookup. Fix: widen the branching factor to shrink height.

cards.question: What makes a B-tree / B+ tree fast?
cards.answer: Each node is sized to a cache line → one cache miss per level. B+ trees keep all values in linked leaves, so a range query is one descent + a linear leaf walk — why every database uses them.

cards.question: Best choice for a mutation-heavy ordered map?
cards.answer: absl::btree_map — a drop-in for std::map, ~3× faster lookup, per-op cost bounded by log N, and no per-node allocation.

cards.question: When do you actually need a priority queue?
cards.answer: When you pop and keep inserting in a loop (event sim, Dijkstra/A*, top-k, scheduling, branch-and-bound). If you just need order once at the end → std::sort.

cards.question: What is a binary heap, physically?
cards.answer: An implicit tree stored in a flat array (std::vector) — parent/child are just index arithmetic. No pointers, no per-node allocations, cache-friendly.

cards.question: How do push and pop work in a heap?
cards.answer: push: append to the end, then bubble up. pop: swap root with the last element, remove it, then sift down to restore heap order.

cards.question: Cheap way to speed up a large heap?
cards.answer: Use a d-ary heap (d = 4 or 8): each node has d children instead of 2. Same array + index math, the d children sit in one cache line, and height drops.

cards.question: How do you 'change a key' in a heap (which it can't do)?
cards.answer: Push a new entry instead (lazy deletion): on pop, skip stale entries whose priority no longer matches. This also kills the last reason to use a Fibonacci heap (cache-hostile in practice).

cards.question: What is union-find and what two tricks make it fast?
cards.answer: Maintains a partition under find(x) and unite(x,y) using just a parent array. Tricks: union by rank (attach shorter tree under taller) + path compression (re-parent nodes to the root during find).

cards.question: What's its real-world cost?
cards.answer: Amortized inverse-Ackermann — which is ≤ 4 for any real input, so treat both ops as effectively O(1). Constant factor is dominated by one hot cache line (the parent array).

cards.question: What's the default graph representation?
cards.answer: Compressed Sparse Row (CSR) — two flat arrays (offsets + targets). Neighbors are contiguous, so traversal streams at memory bandwidth. CSR is to graphs what vector is to lists.

cards.question: Why avoid vector<vector<int>> for a long-lived graph?
cards.answer: Each vertex's neighbors are a separate heap allocation → one cache miss per vertex before you read a single edge. Same pathology as std::list.

cards.question: When is an adjacency matrix the right call?
cards.answer: For small, dense graphs, when edge-existence queries dominate, or when the algorithm is linear algebra (SpMV, matrix powers). Costs V² memory always.

cards.question: How do you pick a spatial structure?
cards.answer: By the query, not the data. Range → grid/R-tree; nearest-neighbor → kd-tree; ray → BVH; collision → BVH/grid. The whole win comes from pruning almost everything.

cards.question: Default spatial structure, and kd-tree's big caveat?
cards.answer: Uniform grid — simple, near-O(1) for near-neighbor queries on roughly uniform points. kd-trees work in low dimensions but degrade to linear scan (~20D) — the curse of dimensionality.

cards.question: Bloom filter — what and what's the catch?
cards.answer: Answers “have I seen it?” in a tiny bit array using k hashes. False positives possible, but never false negatives. ~10 bits/element ≈ 1% error is the usual default.

cards.question: Count-Min sketch vs HyperLogLog — one line each?
cards.answer: Count-Min: approximate frequencies (“how often?”), always an overcount, never under. HyperLogLog: approximate distinct count (“how many unique?”) in kilobytes — powers COUNT(DISTINCT).

lectures.title: Lecture 05 · Optimization Fundamentals
lectures.description: Eight ways to solve optimization problems, all taught through the Knapsack puzzle.

cards.question: How does this lecture's goal differ from the first half of the course?
cards.answer: The first half was about making programs run faster; now the focus shifts to solving optimization problems — finding the best possible answer, not just any answer.

cards.question: Which single problem is used to demonstrate every technique in the lecture?
cards.answer: The Knapsack problem is the running example used to compare all the solving methods.

cards.question: In plain words, what is the Knapsack problem?
cards.answer: You have a backpack with a weight limit and treasures that each have a weight and a value — pick items to get the highest total value without going over the limit.

cards.question: What is the core idea of dynamic programming?
cards.answer: Solve many small subproblems first, store their answers, and reuse them to build up the solution to the big problem.

cards.question: For each item, what are the only two choices dynamic programming compares?
cards.answer: Either skip the item (keep the previous best) or take the item (gain its value but use up bag space) — keep whichever is better.

cards.question: Is dynamic programming usually the best choice in practice?
cards.answer: No — the tables often become too big to be practical. It's common in theory but rarely the best real-world choice; Knapsack is a lucky exception.

cards.question: How does the greedy Knapsack algorithm decide what to pack?
cards.answer: Sort items by value per weight (efficiency) and take them in that order as long as they still fit.

cards.question: Why is the greedy result useful even though it can be imperfect?
cards.answer: It's fast and always a valid, real packing, so it gives a lower bound — a guaranteed "you can do at least this well" (a floor).

cards.question: What extra power does the fractional Knapsack give you?
cards.answer: You may take fractions of items (slice them) to fill the bag exactly to the brim.

cards.question: Why does the fractional solution give an upper bound?
cards.answer: Slicing adds freedom, so it can only be as good or better than the real puzzle — its value is a ceiling the true answer can never beat.

cards.question: What does it mean to "relax" a problem?
cards.answer: A relaxation is an easier version of the problem (like allowing fractions) that gives a useful bound on the real answer.

cards.question: How can all possible Knapsack solutions be pictured?
cards.answer: As a decision tree of yes/no choices — each node is a partial plan and each edge is one "take it / leave it" decision.

cards.question: What is the key move in backtracking?
cards.answer: If a partial plan is already too heavy, stop and discard that whole branch — nothing below it can be valid.

cards.question: What does backtracking achieve versus full brute force?
cards.answer: It prunes impossible branches early, shrinking the search tree instead of exploring every combination.

cards.question: What is constraint propagation?
cards.answer: Using logic to auto-decide forced choices before branching — working out the obvious consequences of what you've already picked.

cards.question: Give an example of a forced choice in Knapsack propagation.
cards.answer: If an item can't possibly fit, automatically leave it; if leftover items are free value and all fit, automatically take them.

cards.question: How does branch & bound go beyond backtracking?
cards.answer: It also discards branches that are possible but can't beat the best answer found so far — not just impossible ones.

cards.question: What key question does branch & bound ask about each branch?
cards.answer: "Could this branch, even in its best case, beat my current record?" If not, prune it.

cards.question: What two values does branch & bound track in order to prune?
cards.answer: A lower bound (best real answer so far) and each branch's upper bound (best it could possibly reach); if the branch's ceiling can't beat your record, drop it.

cards.question: In branch & cut, how is the bound usually computed?
cards.answer: By solving the linear relaxation — dropping the strict "take it or leave it" rule and allowing values in between, which turns the hard problem into an easy one.

cards.question: What is a "cut", and why add one?
cards.answer: A cut is an extra rule that forbids an impossible fractional answer while keeping every real solution legal; it pulls the ceiling closer to reality so branches can be pruned sooner.

cards.question: When do you reach for metaheuristics?
cards.answer: When the problem is too big or too nasty for exact methods to finish — they find a really good answer without promising it's perfect.

cards.question: What are the three big families of metaheuristics?
cards.answer: Constructive heuristics, local search, and population-based algorithms.

cards.question: How does a constructive heuristic build an answer?
cards.answer: It grows a solution step by step (often greedily), possibly with randomness and many repeats, keeping the best attempt.

cards.question: How does local search work?
cards.answer: Start from a solution and repeatedly move to a slightly better neighbor (one small change away) until you can't improve.

cards.question: What is the main danger of local search, and one way to escape it?
cards.answer: Getting stuck in a local optimum (a small hill). Simulated annealing escapes by occasionally allowing a step downhill.

cards.question: What is the exploration vs. exploitation trade-off?
cards.answer: Exploration roams widely to scout new areas, while exploitation polishes what you already have; a good strategy explores first, then intensifies.

cards.question: How do population-based methods work?
cards.answer: Keep a whole population of solutions and each generation combine them, favor the good ones, and keep diversity (e.g. genetic algorithms).

cards.question: What single idea unifies all of these methods?
cards.answer: They are all smarter ways to search the tree of take-it/leave-it choices, trapping the answer between a floor and a ceiling.

cards.question: Why are metaheuristics often the best choice in practice?
cards.answer: Because they scale to huge problems where exact methods can't finish — and they can still team up with exact methods.

lectures.title: Lecture 06 · Bill Cook
lectures.description: No Lecture. Bill Cook was a guest speaker.

lectures.title: Lecture 07 · LP & MIP
lectures.description: How solvers use easy linear programs to crack hard integer problems.

cards.question: What is optimization, in plain terms?
cards.answer: Finding the best plan (e.g. most money) while obeying a set of rules (constraints).

cards.question: What is Linear Programming (LP) and what makes it the 'easy' version?
cards.answer: Maximizing a goal under straight-line rules where answers may be fractions. It's easy because computers can solve it quickly.

cards.question: What is Mixed Integer Programming (MIP) and why is it the 'hard' version?
cards.answer: An LP where some variables must be whole numbers. That single requirement makes it genuinely hard to solve.

cards.question: What is the core trick behind every MIP solver?
cards.answer: Use the easy LP over and over again to slowly crack the hard MIP.

cards.question: What does it mean that 'LP is in P'?
cards.answer: Computers can solve LP quickly (in polynomial time), even for large problems, in both theory and practice.

cards.question: What mainly determines how long an LP takes to solve?
cards.answer: The number of variables, the number of constraints, and how sparse the problem is (how few non-zero coefficients).

cards.question: How does the Simplex algorithm find the answer?
cards.answer: It walks along the edges of the shape of valid answers, checking corners one at a time.

cards.question: What are Simplex's strengths and weaknesses?
cards.answer: Strengths: many cheap steps, great at warm starts, clean solutions. Weaknesses: not proven fast, hard to parallelize.

cards.question: How does the Barrier (Interior Point) method find the answer?
cards.answer: It floats through the middle of the shape and homes in on the answer, instead of crawling along edges.

cards.question: What are Barrier's strengths and weaknesses?
cards.answer: Strengths: proven fast, parallelizable, fewer steps. Weaknesses: each step is expensive, bad at warm starts.

cards.question: What makes PDLP (PDHG) special among LP solvers?
cards.answer: It takes many cheap steps and can even use GPUs, making it very fast to moderate precision.

cards.question: Why is there no single 'best' LP algorithm?
cards.answer: Each one wins in different situations; the right choice depends on the problem's size, structure, and precision needed.

cards.question: What is a 'warm start' and why does it matter?
cards.answer: Reusing a previous solution after a small change, so you only need a few extra steps instead of solving from scratch.

cards.question: What extra rule turns an LP into a MIP?
cards.answer: Some variables are forced to be whole numbers (integers) instead of fractions.

cards.question: What is an Integer Program vs. a Binary Program?
cards.answer: Integer Program: all variables are whole numbers. Binary Program: variables are only 0 or 1 (yes/no switches).

cards.question: What does a {0,1} variable represent?
cards.answer: A yes/no decision, e.g. 'build the bridge?' where 1 = yes and 0 = no.

cards.question: How hard are MIP, IP, and BP as problem classes?
cards.answer: They are all NP-complete — the 'really tough' category of problems.

cards.question: Why does 'whole numbers only' make a problem so hard?
cards.answer: Valid answers become scattered dots instead of a smooth shape, so you can't just slide to the best one.

cards.question: What is the Traveling Salesperson Problem (TSP)?
cards.answer: Visit every city exactly once and return home using the shortest total route.

cards.question: In the treasure-hunt view of solving, what is the Upper Bound (UB)?
cards.answer: The best real, complete route found so far — proof that a route this good exists.

cards.question: In the treasure-hunt view of solving, what is the Lower Bound (relaxation)?
cards.answer: A dream estimate from the easy LP saying no route could possibly be better than this.

cards.question: When do you know you've found the optimal answer?
cards.answer: When the upper bound and lower bound meet at the same value — the answer is then proven best.

cards.question: What is a 'relaxation' of a MIP?
cards.answer: A version where you drop the whole-number rule (and sometimes other rules) so the easy LP can solve it.

cards.question: What problem do cutting planes solve?
cards.answer: The LP gives a fractional answer (like 'use 0.5 of a road') that can't be used in reality.

cards.question: What is a cutting plane, in one idea?
cards.answer: A new rule that slices off the fractional answer without removing any valid whole-number answer.

cards.question: What three properties must a good cutting plane have?
cards.answer: It's linear, satisfied by all valid integer solutions, and violated by the current fractional solution.

cards.question: Why is adding a cut safe and useful?
cards.answer: You keep an easy LP, lose no valid solution, and force a new answer because the old fractional one is cut off.

cards.question: What is the 'separation problem'?
cards.answer: The task of finding a good cutting plane; usually solved by a heuristic that can sometimes fail.

cards.question: What is a comb inequality (TSP)?
cards.answer: A hand-crafted TSP cut using a 'handle' with an odd number of 'teeth' — just a specialized no-cheating rule.

cards.question: What does it mean to 'branch' on a variable?
cards.answer: Take a variable stuck at a fraction and split into two what-if worlds (e.g. round it down vs. round it up), solving each.

cards.question: What is 'pruning' and why is it so powerful?
cards.answer: Throwing away a branch whose best-possible value is already worse than a real answer you have, avoiding huge amounts of work.

cards.question: What is Branch & Cut?
cards.answer: Combining branching and cutting planes — the engine inside every real MIP solver.

cards.question: What is the dual of an LP?
cards.answer: A mirror-image problem (max becomes min) that puts a ceiling on how good any answer could possibly be.

cards.question: What does the Strong Duality Theorem say?
cards.answer: If the primal has an optimal solution, so does the dual, and their optimal values are exactly equal.

cards.question: How does the floor-and-ceiling analogy explain duality?
cards.answer: The primal builds a floor ('at least this high'), the dual sets a ceiling ('no higher than this'), and they meet at the true value.

cards.question: Why does duality matter for MIP solvers?
cards.answer: It's the mathematical proof that when the upper and lower bounds meet, you've truly found the best answer.

cards.question: What is the 'Most Fractional' branching rule?
cards.answer: Branch on the variable closest to halfway between two integers. Simple, but often mediocre.

cards.question: What is Pseudo-Cost Branching?
cards.answer: Keep a running report card per variable estimating how helpful branching on it usually is. Fast but needs data to warm up.

cards.question: What is the main weakness of Pseudo-Cost Branching?
cards.answer: Scores get better over time, but the earliest choices matter most — exactly when scores are least reliable.

cards.question: What is Strong Branching?
cards.answer: Actually test-solving both children for several candidate variables and picking the best. Accurate but expensive.

cards.question: What is Reliability Branching?
cards.answer: A hybrid: use Strong Branching early, then switch to pseudo-costs once they've gathered enough data to be reliable.

cards.question: Why must solvers manage cuts and sometimes 'forget' them?
cards.answer: Too many cuts slow the LP down, so solvers keep a cut pool and drop cuts that aren't helping.

cards.question: What does Presolve do before the real solving starts?
cards.answer: Cleans up the problem — removing useless variables, tightening bounds, rescaling — often the single biggest speedup.

cards.question: What are primal heuristics and why do they help?
cards.answer: Quick tricks to find good real answers fast, giving the upper bounds needed to prune branches.

cards.question: What's the difference between construction and improvement heuristics?
cards.answer: Construction builds a solution from nothing (e.g. diving, feasibility pump); improvement polishes an existing one (e.g. RINS, local branching).

cards.question: What is the Feasibility Pump idea?
cards.answer: Bounce between the LP answer and the nearest rounded point until they line up on a valid solution.

cards.question: What is the idea behind Local Branching / RINS?
cards.answer: Freeze most of a good solution and let the solver re-optimize only a small piece as a mini sub-problem.

cards.question: Summarize how solvers crack a MIP in one sentence.
cards.answer: Repeatedly solve the easy LP to get bounds, then squeeze the upper and lower bounds together using cuts and branching until they meet.
